name: Hypothyroidism
signs:
Fatigue
Weight gain
Cold intolerance
Constipation
Dry skin/hair
Bradycardia
Peripheral edema
Delayed reflexes
Depression
Menorrhagia
Hoarse voice
Myxedema
treatment:
1. Levothyroxine replacement
2. Start low in elderly/cardiac patients
3. Take on empty stomach, 30-60min before breakfast
4. Monitor TSH q6-8 weeks until stable, then annually
5. Adjust dose in pregnancy (increase 25-30%)
meds:
Levothyroxine 1.6mcg/kg/day (full replacement)
Start 25-50mcg daily in elderly or cardiac
Increase by 25mcg q6-8wk to target TSH
Pregnancy: increase dose immediately by 25-30%
ddx: Depression, Anemia, CHF, CKD, Sleep apnea, Chronic fatigue syndrome
labs:
TSH (elevated in primary, low/normal in secondary)
Free T4 (low)
TPO antibodies (Hashimoto)
Lipid panel (often dyslipidemia)
CBC (anemia)
notes:
Hashimoto most common cause in developed world
Subclinical: elevated TSH, normal free T4 - treat if TSH >10 or symptomatic
Myxedema coma: severe hypothyroidism emergency
Drug interactions: calcium, iron, PPI reduce absorption - separate by 4h
Biotin supplements interfere with thyroid lab assays

name: Hyperthyroidism / Graves Disease
signs:
Weight loss despite increased appetite
Heat intolerance
Tremor
Tachycardia/palpitations
Anxiety/irritability
Diarrhea
Exophthalmos (Graves)
Pretibial myxedema (Graves)
Thyroid bruit
Onycholysis
Menstrual irregularity
treatment:
1. Antithyroid drugs (methimazole preferred)
2. Beta-blocker for symptom control
3. Radioactive iodine ablation (definitive)
4. Surgery (thyroidectomy) for large goiter/compressive symptoms
5. Ophthalmology referral for Graves ophthalmopathy
meds:
Methimazole 10-30mg PO daily
PTU 100-200mg TID (first trimester pregnancy only)
Propranolol 20-40mg TID-QID
Atenolol 25-100mg daily
Radioactive iodine I-131 (definitive)
Prednisone for moderate-severe ophthalmopathy
ddx: Anxiety disorder, Pheochromocytoma, Thyroiditis (subacute, painless), Toxic multinodular goiter, Toxic adenoma, Factitious thyrotoxicosis
labs:
TSH (suppressed)
Free T4 and T3 (elevated)
TSI or TRAb (Graves specific)
Radioactive iodine uptake and scan
CBC (check before starting methimazole)
LFTs
notes:
Graves: diffuse uptake on RAIU scan; Toxic nodule: focal hot nodule
Methimazole risk: agranulocytosis (0.2-0.5%) - check WBC if fever/sore throat
PTU risk: hepatotoxicity - only use in first trimester or thyroid storm
Graves ophthalmopathy: may worsen with RAI - give prednisone prophylaxis if moderate
Subclinical hyperthyroidism: low TSH, normal T3/T4 - treat if TSH <0.1 or symptomatic

name: Hyponatremia
signs:
Often asymptomatic if mild/chronic
Nausea/vomiting
Headache
Confusion
Lethargy
Seizures (severe)
Coma (severe)
Respiratory arrest
Muscle cramps
Falls in elderly
treatment:
1. Classify: hypovolemic, euvolemic, hypervolemic
2. Assess severity and chronicity
3. Severe symptoms (seizures, coma): hypertonic saline 3% NaCl
4. Chronic: correct slowly (<=8 mEq/L per 24h)
5. SIADH: fluid restriction, salt tabs, possibly tolvaptan
6. Hypovolemic: NS resuscitation
7. Hypervolemic: fluid/sodium restriction, diuretics, treat underlying
meds:
3% NaCl 100-150mL IV bolus over 10min (repeat x2 if persistent severe symptoms)
NS for hypovolemic
Fluid restriction 1-1.5L/day (SIADH)
Tolvaptan 15mg PO daily (SIADH, not liver disease)
Furosemide + salt tabs (SIADH refractory to fluid restriction)
Demeclocycline 300-600mg BID (SIADH, rarely used)
ddx:
Pseudohyponatremia (hyperlipidemia, hyperproteinemia)
Hypovolemic: diuretics, vomiting, diarrhea, adrenal insufficiency
Euvolemic: SIADH, hypothyroidism, cortisol deficiency
Hypervolemic: CHF, cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome, CKD
labs:
Serum Na, serum osmolality
Urine osmolality, urine Na
BMP
TSH, cortisol (rule out hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency)
LFTs, albumin
Uric acid (low in SIADH)
CXR, CT head (SIADH causes)
notes:
Osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS/CPM): risk if correcting >8-10 mEq/L in 24h in chronic hyponatremia
High risk for ODS: Na <105, alcoholism, malnutrition, liver disease, hypokalemia
SIADH criteria: euvolemic, low serum osm, inappropriately concentrated urine (>100), urine Na >40, normal thyroid/adrenal
Correction rate: <=8 mEq/L per 24h for chronic; can be faster for acute (<48h)
If overcorrected: D5W +/- DDAVP to lower sodium back down

name: Acute Coronary Syndrome - Unstable Angina
signs:
Chest pain at rest or crescendo pattern
New-onset angina with minimal exertion
Similar presentation to NSTEMI
No troponin elevation
ECG may show ST depression, T-wave inversion, or be normal
Dyspnea
Diaphoresis
treatment:
Same as NSTEMI approach:
1. Antiplatelet therapy (aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor)
2. Anticoagulation
3. Risk stratification (TIMI, GRACE)
4. Stress test or coronary angiography based on risk
5. Beta-blocker, statin, nitrates
6. Medical optimization
meds:
Aspirin 325mg then 81mg daily
Clopidogrel 300-600mg load then 75mg daily or Ticagrelor 180mg then 90mg BID
Heparin or enoxaparin
Metoprolol 25-50mg PO BID
Atorvastatin 80mg daily
Nitroglycerin SL or IV
ddx: NSTEMI, Stable angina, GERD, Costochondritis, PE, Anxiety, Aortic dissection
labs:
Serial troponin (negative by definition for UA)
12-lead ECG (serial)
CBC, BMP, lipids
BNP
CXR
Stress test or coronary angiography
notes:
Unstable angina + NSTEMI = NSTE-ACS
With high-sensitivity troponin, true unstable angina is less commonly diagnosed
TIMI risk score guides invasive vs conservative strategy
Difference from NSTEMI: no troponin elevation
High-risk features: recurrent pain, ST changes, hemodynamic instability, high TIMI/GRACE score

name: Mesenteric Ischemia
signs:
Severe abdominal pain out of proportion to exam
Nausea/vomiting
Diarrhea (may be bloody)
Abdominal distension
Bowel sounds initially hyperactive then absent
Peritonitis (late)
Shock (late)
Lactic acidosis
treatment:
1. Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation
2. Broad-spectrum antibiotics
3. Anticoagulation with heparin
4. Emergent CTA
5. Surgical embolectomy or revascularization
6. Endovascular thrombectomy/thrombolysis for selected cases
7. Bowel resection if nonviable bowel
8. Second-look laparotomy in 24-48h
meds:
Heparin 80u/kg bolus then 18u/kg/h
Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h
NS/LR aggressive resuscitation
Vasodilators (papaverine) intra-arterial in some cases
Avoid vasopressors if possible (worsen ischemia)
ddx: SBO, Pancreatitis, AAA rupture, Volvulus, Diverticulitis, Perforated viscus, MI, Aortic dissection
labs:
Lactate (elevated, often >2)
CTA abdomen (filling defect in SMA/SMV, bowel wall changes)
CBC (leukocytosis)
BMP (metabolic acidosis)
LDH (elevated)
D-dimer
ABG (metabolic acidosis)
Amylase/lipase
notes:
Pain out of proportion to exam is classic and should raise suspicion
Acute arterial (SMA embolism most common, often AFib)
Acute venous (MVT: hypercoagulable states)
Non-occlusive (low flow: shock, vasopressors, cardiac failure)
Chronic: postprandial pain, food fear, weight loss (intestinal angina)
Mortality 60-80% if bowel necrosis develops
AFib is most common source of embolism to SMA

name: Toxic Megacolon
signs:
Abdominal distension
Abdominal pain/tenderness
Fever >38.6C
Tachycardia >120
Leukocytosis >10.5k
Anemia
Dehydration
Altered mental status
Decreased bowel sounds
Rebound/guarding (perforation)
treatment:
1. NPO, NGT decompression
2. IV fluids and electrolyte correction
3. IV antibiotics (broad spectrum)
4. Stop anti-motility agents (opioids, anticholinergics, loperamide)
5. Serial abdominal X-rays q12h
6. Knee-chest position, rolling q2h (decompressive)
7. Surgical consultation: colectomy if no improvement in 24-72h or perforation
meds:
Piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g IV q6h or Meropenem 1g IV q8h
Vancomycin 500mg PO/PR QID (if C. diff)
Metronidazole 500mg IV q8h (if C. diff)
IV fluids aggressively
Blood transfusion if anemic
Avoid opioids, anticholinergics
ddx: SBO, Ogilvie syndrome (colonic pseudo-obstruction), Volvulus, Ischemic colitis, Perforated viscus
labs:
Abdominal X-ray (transverse colon >6cm or cecum >9cm)
CBC (leukocytosis, anemia)
BMP
Lactate
Blood cultures
C. diff testing
CT abdomen (if perforation suspected)
Serial KUB
notes:
Definition: radiographic colon dilation + systemic toxicity
Most common causes: IBD (especially UC), C. diff, ischemic colitis
Perforation risk increases with colon diameter and duration
Indicators for surgery: perforation, uncontrolled hemorrhage, progressive dilation despite 48-72h medical therapy
Avoid colonoscopy and barium enema in acute toxic megacolon
Mortality: 20% without perforation, 40%+ with perforation

name: Pulmonary Hypertension
signs:
Progressive dyspnea on exertion
Fatigue
Chest pain
Syncope/presyncope
Peripheral edema
JVD
Loud P2 (pulmonic component of S2)
RV heave
Tricuspid regurgitation murmur
Hepatomegaly
Ascites
treatment:
1. Classify WHO Group (1-5) - treatment differs
2. Group 1 (PAH): pulmonary vasodilators
3. Group 2 (left heart): treat underlying cardiac disease
4. Group 3 (lung disease): treat underlying lung disease, O2
5. Group 4 (CTEPH): pulmonary endarterectomy, riociguat
6. Diuretics for fluid overload
7. Anticoagulation (especially Group 1, Group 4)
8. Supplemental O2
9. Lung transplant for refractory
meds:
Sildenafil 20mg TID or Tadalafil 40mg daily (PDE5i)
Ambrisentan 5-10mg daily (ERA)
Bosentan 62.5-125mg BID (ERA)
Epoprostenol IV continuous (prostacyclin - severe)
Treprostinil SC/IV/inhaled
Riociguat 1-2.5mg TID (sGC stimulator, Group 4)
Furosemide for fluid management
Warfarin (selected patients)
ddx: COPD, CHF, PE (chronic thromboembolic), Interstitial lung disease, Sleep apnea, Valvular heart disease, Connective tissue disease
labs:
Echo (RVSP estimate, RV dilation/dysfunction)
Right heart catheterization (gold standard: mPAP >=20mmHg)
PFTs and DLCO
V/Q scan (rule out CTEPH)
CT chest
6-minute walk test
BNP/NT-proBNP
ANA, HIV, LFTs (connective tissue disease, portopulmonary)
Sleep study if suspected OSA
HRCT
notes:
WHO Groups: 1-PAH, 2-Left heart, 3-Lung disease, 4-CTEPH, 5-Multifactorial
Right heart cath defines PH: mPAP >=20mmHg
Pre-capillary: PAWP <=15; Post-capillary: PAWP >15
Vasoreactivity testing during RHC (if Group 1): CCB if positive
Avoid pregnancy (30-50% mortality)
Epoprostenol: never abruptly discontinue (rebound PH crisis)
Prognosis varies widely by group and functional class

name: Acute Pericardial Effusion
signs:
Dyspnea (most common)
Chest pain/pressure
Muffled heart sounds
Tachycardia
Friction rub (may disappear as fluid accumulates)
JVD
Ewart sign (dullness below left scapula)
Pulsus paradoxus
Hepatomegaly
treatment:
1. Echo-guided pericardiocentesis if hemodynamic compromise
2. NSAIDs + colchicine if inflammatory
3. Drain if large/symptomatic or concern for bacterial/TB
4. Pericardial window for recurrent
5. Treat underlying cause
6. Send pericardial fluid for analysis
meds:
Ibuprofen 600mg TID + Colchicine 0.5mg BID (if inflammatory)
Pericardiocentesis for drainage
Intrapericardial triamcinolone (prevent recurrence)
Antibiotics if purulent
Anti-TB therapy if tuberculous
ddx: Cardiac tamponade, CHF, Constrictive pericarditis, Pleural effusion, MI, PE
labs:
Echo (effusion size, tamponade physiology)
ECG (low voltage, electrical alternans)
CXR (enlarged cardiac silhouette if >250mL)
Pericardial fluid: cell count, protein, LDH, glucose, cytology, culture, ADA, PCR
Blood: CBC, BMP, ESR/CRP
TSH, ANA, HIV
TB testing
notes:
Rate of accumulation matters more than size
Rapid: 150-200mL can cause tamponade
Slow: 1-2L can accumulate without tamponade
Causes: viral/idiopathic, malignancy, uremia, bacterial, TB, autoimmune, post-MI (Dressler), post-cardiac surgery, hypothyroidism
ADA >40 suggests TB in high-prevalence areas
Hemorrhagic: malignancy, TB, uremia, post-procedure

name: Ventricular Tachycardia
signs:
Palpitations
Dizziness/lightheadedness
Syncope
Chest pain
Dyspnea
Hypotension
Wide complex tachycardia on ECG
AV dissociation
Cannon A waves in JVP
Cardiac arrest (pulseless VT)
treatment:
1. Pulseless VT: defibrillation per ACLS protocol
2. Unstable with pulse: synchronized cardioversion
3. Stable monomorphic VT: IV amiodarone or procainamide
4. Polymorphic VT (normal QT): treat as VF - defibrillation + amiodarone
5. Torsades de Pointes: IV magnesium, isoproterenol, overdrive pacing
6. Correct electrolytes (K, Mg)
7. ICD evaluation for secondary prevention
meds:
Amiodarone 150mg IV over 10min then 1mg/min x6h then 0.5mg/min
Procainamide 20-50mg/min until arrhythmia suppressed (max 17mg/kg)
Lidocaine 1-1.5mg/kg IV bolus then 1-4mg/min
Magnesium 2g IV over 5-20min (Torsades)
Isoproterenol 2-10mcg/min (Torsades)
Metoprolol if ischemia-related
ddx: SVT with aberrancy, Pre-excited tachycardia (WPW), Hyperkalemia, Artifact, Ventricular paced rhythm
labs:
12-lead ECG (wide QRS >120ms, rate >100)
BMP (K, Mg, Ca)
Troponin
Magnesium level
Drug levels (digoxin, QT-prolonging meds)
Echo (structural heart disease)
Continuous telemetry
Electrophysiology study (EP study)
notes:
Wide complex tachycardia: treat as VT until proven otherwise
Brugada criteria for differentiating VT from SVT with aberrancy
AV dissociation, fusion beats, capture beats = VT
Common causes: ischemia/MI, structural heart disease, electrolyte abnormalities, drugs
Torsades: polymorphic VT + prolonged QT interval
QT-prolonging drugs: antiarrhythmics, antibiotics (macrolides, fluoroquinolones), antipsychotics, methadone
ICD indicated for survivors of VT/VF arrest (secondary prevention)

name: Aortic Stenosis
signs:
Exertional dyspnea
Angina
Syncope/presyncope
Crescendo-decrescendo systolic murmur (RUSB, radiates to carotids)
Diminished and delayed carotid upstroke (pulsus parvus et tardus)
Narrow pulse pressure
S4 gallop
Heart failure (late)
treatment:
1. Symptomatic severe AS: valve replacement (TAVR or SAVR)
2. Asymptomatic severe: monitor, exercise testing, intervene if EF drops
3. Medical management limited (does not change natural history)
4. Avoid excessive preload/afterload reduction
5. Balloon valvuloplasty as bridge to definitive therapy
6. TAVR preferred in intermediate-high surgical risk
meds:
Limited medical therapy:
Cautious diuretics for fluid overload
Avoid vasodilators (nitrates, ACEi) in severe AS - can cause syncope
Statins do NOT slow progression
Beta-blockers cautiously if needed
IE prophylaxis for prosthetic valves
ddx: HOCM, Subaortic membrane, Mitral regurgitation, Aortic sclerosis (no gradient), VSD
labs:
Echo (valve area, mean gradient, peak velocity)
Severe: AVA <1.0cm2, mean gradient >40mmHg, peak velocity >4m/s
ECG (LVH, LAE)
CXR (calcified aortic valve, cardiomegaly)
CT calcium scoring
Cardiac catheterization (if discrepancy)
BNP
notes:
Classic triad: angina, syncope, heart failure
Once symptomatic, prognosis poor without intervention:
Angina: 5yr survival; Syncope: 3yr; Heart failure: 2yr
Bicuspid aortic valve: most common cause in <70; Degenerative calcific in >70
Low-flow low-gradient AS: dobutamine stress echo to differentiate true-severe from pseudo-severe
TAVR: transcatheter aortic valve replacement - preferred route for most patients now

name: Acute Mesenteric Ischemia
signs:
Acute severe abdominal pain
Pain out of proportion to physical exam
Bloody diarrhea (late)
Nausea/vomiting
Abdominal distension
Minimal tenderness initially
Peritonitis (late, ominous)
Shock
notes: Included for search - see Mesenteric Ischemia for full details

name: Nephrotic Syndrome
signs:
Severe peripheral edema (pitting)
Periorbital edema
Anasarca
Foamy urine (proteinuria)
Weight gain
Fatigue
Dyspnea (pleural effusions)
Ascites
Hyperlipidemia
Infection susceptibility
treatment:
1. Treat underlying cause
2. ACEi or ARB (reduce proteinuria)
3. Sodium restriction <2g/day
4. Diuretics for edema (loop +/- thiazide)
5. Statin for hyperlipidemia
6. Anticoagulation if high VTE risk (especially membranous)
7. Immunosuppression based on biopsy results
8. Pneumococcal vaccination
meds:
Lisinopril 10-40mg daily or Losartan 50-100mg daily
Furosemide 40-80mg PO BID + Metolazone 2.5-5mg (if resistant)
Atorvastatin 40-80mg daily
Albumin 25% IV + furosemide (severe edema with low albumin)
Prednisone 1mg/kg/day (minimal change disease)
Cyclophosphamide, rituximab, tacrolimus (based on histology)
ddx: Nephritic syndrome, CHF, Cirrhosis, Protein-losing enteropathy, Malnutrition, Myxedema
labs:
Urinalysis (3-4+ protein, lipiduria, oval fat bodies)
24h urine protein >3.5g/day (or spot protein/Cr >3.5)
Serum albumin (<3g/dL)
Lipid panel (elevated)
BMP (renal function)
Complement levels (C3, C4)
ANA, anti-PLA2R (membranous), Hep B/C, HIV
Renal biopsy (definitive for classification)
notes:
Nephrotic vs Nephritic: Nephrotic = massive proteinuria, hypoalbuminemia, edema, hyperlipidemia; Nephritic = hematuria, HTN, mild proteinuria, RBC casts
Most common in adults: Membranous nephropathy, FSGS, Minimal change disease
Most common in children: Minimal change disease
VTE risk: loss of antithrombin III, especially membranous
SBP risk: loss of immunoglobulins
Anti-PLA2R positive = primary membranous

name: Urolithiasis / Renal Colic
signs:
Severe colicky flank pain radiating to groin
Hematuria (gross or microscopic)
Nausea/vomiting
Restlessness (cannot find comfortable position)
CVA tenderness
Urinary frequency/urgency
Dysuria
Testicular/labial pain (referred)
treatment:
1. Pain management (NSAIDs first-line + opioids if needed)
2. Hydration
3. Alpha-blocker for medical expulsive therapy (stones 5-10mm)
4. <5mm: likely to pass spontaneously (>90%)
5. 5-10mm: medical expulsive therapy, may need intervention
6. >10mm or failed passage: urologic intervention (SWL, ureteroscopy, PCNL)
7. Emergent: infected obstructed stone = urology STAT
meds:
Ketorolac 30mg IV/15mg IM (first-line analgesic)
Ibuprofen 600mg PO q8h
Morphine 0.1mg/kg IV or Hydromorphone 0.5-1mg IV PRN
Tamsulosin 0.4mg PO daily (medical expulsive therapy)
Nifedipine 30mg PO daily (alternative MET)
Ondansetron 4mg IV PRN
ddx: Pyelonephritis, Appendicitis, Ovarian torsion, Ectopic pregnancy, AAA, Musculoskeletal back pain, Renal infarction
labs:
Urinalysis (hematuria in 85%, r/o infection)
Urine culture
BMP (creatinine, calcium)
CBC (leukocytosis if infected)
CT abdomen/pelvis without contrast (gold standard)
US (first-line in pregnancy, children)
Strain urine for stone analysis
24h urine collection (recurrent stone formers)
notes:
Stone composition: Calcium oxalate (80%), Uric acid (10%), Struvite (5-10%), Cystine (1%)
Infected obstructed stone = EMERGENCY - sepsis risk, needs emergent decompression (stent or nephrostomy)
Uric acid stones: radiolucent on X-ray, seen on CT
Struvite: staghorn calculi, associated with urease-producing organisms (Proteus)
Prevention: hydration (>2.5L/day), dietary modification based on stone type
Recurrence rate ~50% within 5-10 years without prevention

name: Spinal Cord Compression
signs:
Back pain (often first symptom)
Weakness/paralysis below level of compression
Sensory level
Bowel/bladder dysfunction (urinary retention, incontinence)
Saddle anesthesia (cauda equina)
Hyperreflexia below lesion (UMN) or hyporeflexia (LMN/cauda equina)
Babinski sign positive
treatment:
1. MRI whole spine STAT
2. High-dose dexamethasone if malignant compression
3. Neurosurgical consultation
4. Radiation oncology consultation for malignant
5. Surgical decompression (emergency if progressive deficit)
6. Foley catheter if urinary retention
7. DVT prophylaxis
8. Treat underlying cause
meds:
Dexamethasone 10mg IV bolus then 4mg IV q6h (malignant compression)
Pain management: scheduled acetaminophen + opioids PRN
Gabapentin 300mg TID (neuropathic pain)
Enoxaparin 40mg SC daily (DVT prophylaxis after surgery)
ddx: Transverse myelitis, Epidural abscess, Epidural hematoma, Disc herniation, GBS, Multiple sclerosis, Spinal infarction
labs:
MRI whole spine with contrast (gold standard)
Plain X-rays (may show lytic/blastic lesions, loss of pedicle)
CT myelography if MRI contraindicated
CBC, BMP, ESR/CRP
PSA, SPEP (if unknown primary)
Post-void residual (urinary retention)
Blood cultures if abscess suspected
notes:
Oncologic emergency: malignant cord compression, typically metastatic (lung, breast, prostate, renal, myeloma)
Cauda equina syndrome: saddle anesthesia, urinary retention, bilateral leg weakness/pain - surgical emergency
Surgical decompression within 24-48h gives best outcomes
If cannot get MRI within 4h and high suspicion, start dexamethasone empirically
Epidural abscess: fever + back pain + neuro deficit = MRI STAT (S. aureus most common)

name: Diabetic Emergencies - Hypoglycemia
signs:
Tremor
Diaphoresis
Palpitations
Anxiety
Hunger
Confusion
Slurred speech
Seizures
Loss of consciousness
Focal neurologic deficits (may mimic stroke)
Coma
treatment:
1. Conscious patient: oral glucose (15-20g fast-acting carb)
2. Unconscious/unable to swallow: IV dextrose or IM glucagon
3. Recheck glucose q15min
4. Once glucose >70, give complex carb/meal
5. Identify and treat cause
6. Adjust diabetes medications
7. Observe: sulfonylurea-induced hypoglycemia may recur (long half-life)
meds:
D50 (50% dextrose) 25-50mL IV (12.5-25g glucose)
Glucagon 1mg IM/SC/IN (if no IV access)
Oral glucose tablets 15-20g
D10 infusion for sustained hypoglycemia
Octreotide 50mcg SC q6-8h (sulfonylurea-induced, refractory)
ddx: Stroke, Seizure disorder, Syncope, Intoxication, Sepsis, Adrenal insufficiency, Insulinoma
labs:
Point-of-care glucose STAT
BMP
Insulin level, C-peptide, proinsulin (if cause unclear)
Sulfonylurea screen
Cortisol
LFTs
Beta-hydroxybutyrate
notes:
Whipple triad: symptoms of hypoglycemia + low glucose + resolution with glucose correction
Sulfonylureas: long-acting, may cause prolonged hypoglycemia - observe 24h, octreotide helpful
Hypoglycemia unawareness: recurrent hypoglycemia blunts counter-regulatory response
Non-diabetic hypoglycemia workup: insulin, C-peptide, proinsulin, sulfonylurea screen during episode
High C-peptide + high insulin = endogenous (insulinoma, sulfonylurea)
Low C-peptide + high insulin = exogenous insulin

name: Pneumocystis Pneumonia (PCP)
signs:
Progressive dyspnea over days-weeks
Dry nonproductive cough
Fever
Tachypnea
Hypoxemia (often out of proportion to exam)
Crackles (may be subtle)
Weight loss
Malaise
treatment:
1. TMP-SMX (first-line)
2. Adjunctive corticosteroids if PaO2 <70 or A-a gradient >35
3. Supplemental oxygen
4. Start ART if HIV-positive (may wait 2 weeks to reduce IRIS risk)
5. Duration: 21 days for HIV, 14 days for non-HIV
6. Secondary prophylaxis until CD4 >200 for 3+ months
meds:
TMP-SMX 15-20mg/kg/day (TMP component) IV/PO divided q6-8h x21d
Prednisone 40mg BID x5d, then 40mg daily x5d, then 20mg daily x11d (if PaO2 <70)
Alternatives: Pentamidine 4mg/kg IV daily, Atovaquone 750mg PO BID, Clindamycin 600mg IV q6h + Primaquine 30mg PO daily
Prophylaxis: TMP-SMX DS 1 tab daily
ddx: Community-acquired pneumonia, TB, CMV pneumonitis, Lymphocytic interstitial pneumonitis, Pulmonary Kaposi sarcoma, Fungal pneumonia
labs:
CXR (bilateral diffuse GGO/interstitial infiltrates)
CT chest (GGOs, crazy paving)
LDH (often markedly elevated)
ABG (hypoxemia, elevated A-a gradient)
Beta-D-glucan (elevated, sensitive)
Induced sputum or BAL for PCP PCR/DFA
CD4 count
HIV viral load
notes:
CD4 <200 = risk for PCP (most common AIDS-defining illness)
Can also occur in non-HIV immunosuppression (transplant, chronic steroids, chemotherapy)
LDH elevation correlates with disease severity
Beta-D-glucan: >80pg/mL supports diagnosis (also elevated in other fungal infections)
CXR may be normal in early disease
Pneumothorax is a known complication
Spontaneous pneumothorax in HIV patient = think PCP

name: Tuberculosis
signs:
Chronic cough >2-3 weeks
Hemoptysis
Night sweats
Weight loss
Fever (low grade)
Fatigue
Anorexia
Lymph node enlargement
Apical lung findings (crackles, decreased BS)
treatment:
1. Isolate (airborne precautions, negative pressure room)
2. Active TB: 4-drug regimen (RIPE) x2mo then INH+RIF x4mo
3. MDR-TB: individualized based on sensitivities
4. Latent TB: INH x9mo or Rifampin x4mo or INH+Rifapentine x12 weekly doses
5. DOT (directly observed therapy) recommended
6. Report to public health
meds:
Isoniazid (INH) 5mg/kg (max 300mg) daily
Rifampin 10mg/kg (max 600mg) daily
Pyrazinamide 25mg/kg daily
Ethambutol 15-20mg/kg daily
Pyridoxine (B6) 25-50mg daily (prevent INH neuropathy)
Latent: INH 300mg daily x9mo + B6
ddx: Lung cancer, Pneumonia, MAC, Fungal infection (histo, cocci, blasto), Sarcoidosis, NTM
labs:
CXR (upper lobe cavitary lesion, infiltrate, effusion, miliary pattern)
Sputum AFB smear x3 (early morning)
Sputum mycobacterial culture (gold standard, takes weeks)
Sputum GeneXpert MTB/RIF (rapid PCR, 2 hours)
QuantiFERON-TB Gold or PPD (latent TB screening)
CBC, CMP, HIV test
LFTs (baseline before treatment)
notes:
Airborne transmission - N95 respirator required
Cavitary disease: most infectious
Miliary TB: hematogenous spread, tiny nodules throughout lungs
INH side effects: hepatotoxicity, peripheral neuropathy (give B6)
Rifampin: orange discoloration of fluids, CYP450 inducer (many drug interactions)
Ethambutol: optic neuritis (check visual acuity)
Pyrazinamide: hepatotoxicity, hyperuricemia
Treat latent TB before starting anti-TNF therapy
